name: The rejection at Nazareth
notes: Matthew and Mark recount the same visit of Jesus to his own country, where he taught in the synagogue and the townsfolk, though astonished at his wisdom and mighty works, took offence because they knew his family. The shared core runs in parallel: his coming home and teaching, the people's astonished question about the source of his wisdom and works, their pointing to the carpenter and his named kin, their being offended, his proverb that a prophet lacks honour in his own country and house, and the few mighty works done there because of their unbelief. The two keep complementary vantages: Matthew names him 'the carpenter's son' and reports he 'did not many mighty works there,' while Mark calls him 'the carpenter, the son of Mary,' adds that his disciples followed and that he yet laid hands on a few sick folk, and says plainly he 'could there do no mighty work,' marvelling at their unbelief. Luke 4:16-30 records a fuller, earlier visit to Nazareth (the reading of Isaiah, the proverb 'Physician, heal thyself,' and the attempt to cast him from the brow of the hill) that is often treated as a separate occasion; it is intentionally not linked here.

links.label: taught them in their synagogue ... began to teach in the synagogue
Whence hath this man this wisdom and these mighty works ... what wisdom is this ... such mighty works

James and Joses and Simon and Judas ... James and Joses and of Juda and Simon
his sisters are they not all with us ... are not his sisters here with us
they were offended in him ... they were offended at him
A prophet is not without honour save in his own country and in his own house
he did not many mighty works there ... he could there do no mighty work
